topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation felt like a deliberate slight. I smoothed down my jacket, the fabric suddenly feeling too tight across my shoulders. Marcus, the architect of my most awkward middle school years, was hosting a small, intimate gathering. My stomach tightened into a knot of resentment as I stood by the doorway, observing the easy camaraderie. He looked… composed. Too composed. He offered me a nod, a brief, almost clinical acknowledgment, and then turned to engage another guest in what sounded like a deep, thoughtful exchange. I took a slow breath, the air catching in my throat. *He actually cares about people now?* The thought was acidic. I forced a smile that didn't reach my eyes, a thin, brittle thing. I retreated to the periphery, my gaze sweeping over the room, cataloging every subtle gesture, searching for the familiar spark of the boy who used to push me into the lockers. It wasn't there. Instead, there was this polished, professional distance that made my jaw clench.

[story 2]

I watched him from across the room, a glass of sparkling water cool in my hand. He was speaking softly, his hands gesturing with an unnerving gentleness as he spoke to a nervous-looking woman. Every word he uttered seemed weighted, precise, devoid of the rough edges I remembered from our shared playground battles. A sharp, unpleasant sensation rose in my chest, like a sudden cramp. I shifted my weight, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, a physical barrier against the warmth of the room. I wanted to scoff, to make some cutting remark about his newfound sophistication, but the words dissolved before they reached my lips. Instead, I focused on the way his eyes crinkled at the corners when he listened, the genuine focus in his gaze. It was infuriating. He had transformed himself into something refined, something untouchable, and it only amplified the feeling of being utterly out of place, a stain on his carefully curated image.

[story 3]

"It's good to see you, Alex," Marcus said, his voice modulated, warm, and utterly devoid of the sharp edge I was accustomed to. I managed a curt nod, my expression carefully neutral, though I felt a distinct tightening in my throat. I stood awkwardly near the edge of the gathering, feeling acutely aware of my own ill-fitting clothes. He offered a hand, and when I shook it, his grip was firm, steady—the kind of steady that suggested control. I pulled my hand back a fraction too quickly. *He’s playing a role,* I thought, the conviction heavy and bitter. I watched him later, observing how he listened intently, how he validated others with an unnerving empathy. It felt like a performance, a carefully constructed facade, and the sheer effort required to maintain that serenity made my own agitation feel loud and clumsy by comparison. I just wanted him to slip up, to show me the familiar, irritating roughness that used to define him.
